coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric, visual, dual-signal fluorescent sensing and imaging of pH/copper ions in real samples based on carbon dots-fluorescein isothiocyanate composites
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Xinxin
Jin, Hui
Gao, Cuili
Gui, Rijun
Wang, Zonghua
coredata.dc:description: In this article, a facile aqueous synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) was developed by using natural kelp as a new carbon source. Through hydrothermal carbonization of kelp juice, fluorescent CDs were prepared and the CDs’ surface was modified with polyethylenimine (PEI). The PEI-modified CDs were conjugated with fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) to fabricate CDs-FITC composites. To exploit broad applications, the CDs-FITC composites were developed as fluorescent sensing or imaging platforms of pH and Cu2+. Analytical performances of the composites-based fluorescence (FL) sensors were evaluated, including visual FL imaging of pH in glass bottle, ratiometric FL sensing of pH in yogurt samples, visual FL latent fingerprint and leaf imaging detection of [Cu2+], dual-signal FL sensing of [Cu2+] in yogurt and human serum samples. Experimental results from ratiometric, visual, dual-signal FL sensing and imaging applications confirmed the high feasibility, accuracy, stabilization and simplicity of CDs-FITC composites-based FL sensors for the detection of pH and Cu2+ ions in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescein isothiocyanate
Ratiometric
Latent fingerprint imaging
Visual detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs and CDs-FITC composites
Absorption and FL spectral properties of CDs and CDs-FITC composites
Visual FL imaging detection of pH and ratiometric FL intensity sensing of pH
Analytical performance of ratiometric FL sensor of pH in real samples
FL dual-signal intensity sensing and visual FL imaging detection of Cu2+
Analytical performance of FL dual-signal sensor of Cu2+ in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images, (b) FT-IR spectrum, XRD patterns and (d) Raman spectrum of the CDs prepared from hydrothermal carbonization of kelp juice.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum, FL excitation (Ex.) and FL emission (Em.) spectra of CDs. (b) FL emission spectra of CDs under excitation at different wavelengths. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra and (d) FL emission spectra from CDs, FITC and CDs-FITC composites.

(a) FL images of the aqueous suspension of CDs-FITC composites dispersed in PBS at different pHs, excited by a 365nm of UV-lamp. (b) FL emission spectra of CDs-FITC composites dispersed in PBS at different pHs, excited at 340nm. (c) Ratiometric FL intensities (I FITC/I CDs) of composites at the maximum emission peaks of FITC and CDs, under different pHs. (d) The linear plotting of I FITC/I CDs versus pH.

(a) FL emission spectra of CDs-FITC composites in the presence of Cu2+, excited at 340nm. (b) Relative FL intensities (F0-F)/F0 (F0 and F stand for FL intensities before and after the addition of Cu2+) in the FL emission spectra of composites peaked at 412nm (from CDs) and at 518nm (from FITC) versus different [Cu2+]. (c) FL intensities peaked at 412nm (CDs) and at 518nm (FITC), in the presence of each class of cations (12μM). (d) CDs-FITC composites with the addition of 0, 2 and 5μM Cu2+ for FL imaging in latent fingerprints, and with the addition of 0, 2, 5, 10μM Cu2+ for FL imaging in leaves, excited with a 365nm of UV-lamp.
Schematic representation of the preparation processes and applications of CDs-FITC composites for ratiometric, visual and dual-signal FL sensing of pH and Cu2+ ions.
Analytical results of pHs in 50-fold diluted yogurt samples from the ratiometric FL sensor and pH-meter.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Results were expressed as the mean of six determinations±standard deviation (SD).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Relative standard deviation (RSD) was defined as (SD/mean)×100%.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical results of [Cu2+] in 50-fold diluted real samples from the FL dual-signal sensor (based on the linear plottings at 412nm from CDs and 518nm from FITC) and ICP-AES.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Results were expressed as the mean of six determinations±standard deviation (SD).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Relative standard deviation (RSD) was defined as (SD/mean)×100%.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric, visual, dual-signal fluorescent sensing and imaging of pH/copper ions in real samples based on carbon dots-fluorescein isothiocyanate composites

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Sino-Japanese Center for Collaborative Research of Carbon Nanomaterials, Collaborative Innovation Center for Marine Biomass Fiber Materials and Textiles, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Laboratory of Fiber Materials and Modern Textile, The Growing Base for State Key Laboratory, Qingdao University, Shandong 266071, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Sino-Japanese Center for Collaborative Research of Carbon Nanomaterials, Collaborative Innovation Center for Marine Biomass Fiber Materials and Textiles, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Laboratory of Fiber Materials and Modern Textile, The Growing Base for State Key Laboratory, Qingdao University, Shandong 266071

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors have the equivalent contribution to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this article, a facile aqueous synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) was developed by using natural kelp as a new carbon source. Through hydrothermal carbonization of kelp juice, fluorescent CDs were prepared and the CDs’ surface was modified with polyethylenimine (PEI). The PEI-modified CDs were conjugated with fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) to fabricate CDs-FITC composites. To exploit broad applications, the CDs-FITC composites were developed as fluorescent sensing or imaging platforms of pH and Cu2+. Analytical performances of the composites-based fluorescence (FL) sensors were evaluated, including visual FL imaging of pH in glass bottle, ratiometric FL sensing of pH in yogurt samples, visual FL latent fingerprint and leaf imaging detection of [Cu2+], dual-signal FL sensing of [Cu2+] in yogurt and human serum samples. Experimental results from ratiometric, visual, dual-signal FL sensing and imaging applications confirmed the high feasibility, accuracy, stabilization and simplicity of CDs-FITC composites-based FL sensors for the detection of pH and Cu2+ ions in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: During the past decade, carbon dots (CDs) as a promising class of carbon nanomaterials have attracted numerous attention due to their unique properties, such as broad excitation, size-tunable fluorescence, high water-solubility, facile synthesis, ease of functionalization, chemical stability, low environmental hazard, etc [1–4]. These superior properties of CDs significantly promote their extensive applications especially in chemical/biomedical sensing and imaging fields [5–7]. The synthesis of fluorescent CDs can be generally classified into two types: including top-down and bottom-up methods [8]. As for the top-down methods, usually CDs are formed by post-treating of carbon particles, broken from a larger carbon structure by means of arc discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, etc [9,10]. Bottom-up methods of CDs focus on thermal carbonization, hydrothermal, photochemical reduction, acid dehydration, ultrasonic treatment, etc [11–13]. By contrast, the method of hydrothermal carbonization has obvious superiorities due to its simplicity, low cost, low temperature and time-saving processes, and so far it has been widely developed as a facile, classical and eco-friendly method for the synthesis of fluorescent CDs [14].

Interestingly, in recent years fluorescent CDs have been synthesized by means of hydrothermal carbonization methods, using “green” raw materials as carbon sources, such as aloe, pomelo peel, watermelon peel, sweet pepper, orange juice, etc [14–18]. Based on the point of material synthesis, to date the exploitation of new carbon sources from readily available natural products for simple and economical synthesis of water-dispersible fluorescent CDs has become an urgent requirement. Herein, in this article, a facile synthesis of CDs was developed by using natural kelp as a new type of carbon sources. After only hydrothermal carbonization of kelp juice for 5h at 180°C, water- dispersible fluorescent CDs were obtained directly. The surface of as-obtained CDs was modified with polyethylenimine (PEI) to form –NH2 terminated CDs/PEI composites ( Scheme 1), which were then conjugated with pH- and Cu2+-sensitive fluorescence dyes (fluorescein isothiocyanate, FITC) to facilely fabricate the CDs-FITC composites under electrostatic interactions and thiourea bonds in aqueous media [19–21].

To date, only three literatures have referred to the researches on CDs-FITC composites [22–24]. Yang et al. prepared FA-CS-FITC(DOX/C-dots)/VEGF shRNA hybrids as the theranostic platform [22]. Ma et al. prepared CD@Nile-FITC@Gel hybrids for cell tracking [23]. Both literatures have referred to CDs-FITC composites. However, the corresponding hybrids with multiple components and structures are relatively complicated to prepare. Besides, Du et al. prepared the CDs-FITC composites through covalently linking FITC onto the surface of CDs for intracellular pH sensing and mapping [24]. To further exploit potential applications in promising fields, in this article we attempted to investigate the ratiometric, visual and dual-signal fluorescent sensing and imaging performances of CDs-FITC composites toward pH and Cu2+ ions in real samples. In detail, we intended to use the as-prepared CDs-FITC composites for ratiometric and visual fluorescence (FL) sensing of pH, and the pH determination in yogurt samples. Additionally, we also intended to use CDs-FITC composites for visual and dual-signal FL sensing of Cu2+ ions in yogurt and human serum samples, and FL imaging of Cu2+ ions in leaves and latent fingerprints. Owing to the facile preparation, efficient (ratiometric, visual and dual-signal) FL sensing and imaging applications, these researches on the promising CDs-FITC composites would promote the development in facile synthetic methods and broad applications of CDs-based composites consisting of dual or multiple components and microstructures.

Kelp was obtained from a certain vegetable market of Qingdao, China. PEI (M.W. 1800, 99%) and FITC (95%) were purchased from Aladdin. HCl, NaOH, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, CuCl2, ethanol and acetone were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Other chemicals were of analytical grade, and all chemicals were utilized directly as received without purification. Distilled water was used in all experiments.

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2800A spectrophotometer (Unic, Shanghai). Fluorescence emission spectra were measured on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian Co. Ltd., USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) measurements on powdered samples were made by using a Nicolet 5700 FTIR spectrometer in transmission mode with a KBr window. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were characterized by using a DX-2700 powder X-ray diffractometer with graphite monochromatized Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.54056Å). TEM images were recorded on a Tecnai F20 microscope (FEI, USA). A draught drying cabinet (DHG-9240A), a circumrotation evaporation (RE52CS-2) and a highspeed centrifuge (TG1650-WS) were utilized for the preparation and purification of water-dispersible CDs. Raman spectra were measured by a Horiba spectrometer, using a 10× objective lens with 90s acquisition time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and CDs-FITC composites
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of CDs, briefly 5g of fresh and clean kelp was added into 20mL of distilled water, followed by breaking fresh kelp into kelp juice. The kelp juice (10mL) was transferred into a 50mL Teflon-lined autoclave, which was heated for 5h at 180°C. Afterward, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The reaction products were filtered with a 0.22µm membrane, and the filtration solution was washed with dichloromethane to remove unreacted organic components. The upper solution was treated by circumrotating evaporation and centrifugation, and the resulting precipitates were washed by ethanol. After twice repeated washing treatment and centrifugation, the final precipitates were collected and dried at 60°C to obtain the solid samples of CDs, or were dispersed in distilled water to obtain the aqueous suspension of CDs for further use in following experiments.

For the synthesis of CDs-FITC composites, briefly the as-obtained CDs were dispersed in 10mL of phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 10mM, pH 7.0), and the concentration of CDs was fixed to be 1.0mgmL−1. Under stirring and ultrasonication, 5mL of PEI (0.1wt%) and 5mL of FITC (0.1wt%) dispersed in PBS were drop-by-drop injected into the aqueous suspension of CDs in turn to obtain the homogeneous aqueous suspension of CDs-FITC composites in PBS. The purification procedures of CDs-FITC composites were similar to that of CDs. Finally, the resulting dried and aqueous suspension samples of composites were used for subsequent experiments.

To realize CDs-FITC composites-based sensing of pH, CDs-FITC composites were dispersed in PBS and the final pHs were adjuated to different values (3.2, 3.6, 3.8, 4.1, 4.3, 4.9, 5.6, 5.9, 6.1, 6.5, 7.0, 7.4, 7.7, 8.0, 8.4, 8.8 and 9.0) under similar preparation conditions to the case at pH 7.0 (in Section 2.2). As a result, a series of PBS aqueous suspensions of composites with different pHs were prepared and used to estimate the pH-senstive FL responses of CDs-FITC composites by measuring FL emission spectra. For the case at pH 7.0, the resulting CDs-FITC composites with the addition of Cu2+ ions (0–13μM) were utilized to evaluate the Cu2+-senstive FL responses. In addition, the CDs-FITC composites (in PBS at pH 7.0) were treated with other cations, including Hg2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Na+, K+, Li+, Ag+ and NH4+(each one with a concentration of 12μM). Different FL emission spectra of the composites with the addition of different cations were measured respectively.

In a typical experiment, yogurt samples with 50-fold filtration with PBS (10mM, pH 7.0) were mixed with the aqueous suspension of CDs-FITC composites. In the sample-sensor mixture, the concentration of composites was regulated to be equal to that of composites prepared at pH 7.0 (in Section 2.2). Different doses of CuCl2 were added into 50-fold diluted yogurt samples, and the resulting mixture solutions was then mixed with the aqueous suspension of CDs-FITC composites to form a series of homogeneous solutions. Therein, the concentration of CDs-FITC composites was regulated to be equal to that of composites prepared at pH 7.0. Furthermore, for the aqueous suspension of CDs-FITC composites with different pHs, the visual FL images of different pHs in glass bottle were recorded. The ratiometric FL measurements of pH in yogurt samples were carried out. For the aqueous suspension of CDs-FITC composites (in PBS at pH 7.0) containing different Cu2+ concentrations [Cu2+], the ratiometric FL measurements of [Cu2+] in yogurt and human serum samples were performed. The visual FL images of [Cu2+] in leaves and latent fingerprints were conducted respectively.

In this article, fluorescent CDs were facilely prepared by using natural kelp as a new class of carbon sources. Through direct hydrothermal carbonization of kelp juice for 5h at 180°C, CDs were achieved. In Fig. 1a, the as-prepared CDs showed a regular spherical morphology and a narrow diameter distribution of 3–7nm. The average diameter is approximately 4.5nm, which is very close to that of CDs prepared from hydrothermal carbonization of other natrual products [14–18]. To identify the surface functional groups of CDs, FT-IR spectrum of CDs was measured and shown in Fig. 1b. Clearly, there is a characteristic absorption band at 3372cm−1, which is assigned to the O-H stretching vibration mode. The C-H stretching vibration band appears at 2961cm−1, while the stretching vibration of –OH is indicated by the strong absorption bands at 3372 and 1056cm−1. The intensive bands at 1594 and 1387cm−1 are assigned to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of –COO-. These results suggest the presence of –COOH and –OH groups around the surface of CDs, which therefore reveals that the as-prepared CDs are negatively charged in aqueous media. On this account, the water-dispersible CDs can perform a facile combination with PEI to form the –NH2 terminated CDs/PEI composites, followed by the combination of CDs/PEI composites with FITC to facilely fabricate the CDs-FITC composites under electrostatic interactions [18,25]. The powder XRD patterns of the as-prepared CDs were measured to investigate the crystallinity of CDs. As exhibited in Fig. 1c, a broad amorphous peak appears at 22.5° (2-therta), indicating the amorphous feature of CDs [18,26,27]. Raman spectrum of the CDs was recorded to investigate the existence of aromatic carbons. In Fig. 1d, there are two clear bands at 1579 and 1370cm−1, which should be respectively assigned to the G-band (sp2) and d-band (sp3). Generally, G-band implies the vibration mode of a sp2 carbon atom, while d-band implies a vibration mode of carbon atom in a sp3 hybridized orbital. The obvious G- and d-bands indicate two different classes of carbon species with comparable amounts in the as-prepared CDs. The sp2 carbon atoms are considered as the aromatic segments in CDs [26,28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption and FL spectral properties of CDs and CDs-FITC composites
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of as-prepared CDs were evaluated by measuring UV–vis absorption and FL spectra of CDs. In Fig. 2a, there is a characteristic absorption peak located at ~340nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs. The FL excitation spectrum of CDs exhibits a broad range of excitation wavelength, while the FL emission spectrum shows a maximum emission peak at 410nm under excitation at a wavelength of 340nm. The quantum yield of CDs was calculated to be 12.3% by using rhodamine B as a standard [29]. The FL properties of CDs were further researched at gradually increasing excitation wavelengths. In Fig. 2b, a marked red-shift was observed in the FL emission spectra of CDs with the excitation wavelength increasing from 340nm to 420nm. It indicates that the FL of CDs is strongly dependent on the corresponding excitation wavelength, in good agreement with previous reports on fluorescent CDs [14,16,18]. The excitation wavelength- dependent FL emission behavior might be attributed to the different size distribution of CDs and different emissive sites on each carbon dot, as well as multiphoton active processes [18]. Fig. 2c displays a series of UV–vis absorption spectra from CDs, FITC and CDs-FITC composites. For the CDs-FITC composites, two marked absorption peaks were observed, which are consistent with the characteristic absorption peaks from CDs and FITC. In addition, the FL emission spectrum (Fig. 2d) of the composites also show two obvious FL emission peaks, which are in agreement with the characteristic emission peaks from CDs and FITC. According to the experimental processes (as described in Section 2.2) and measured spectral results (in Fig. 2), the as-characterized composites should be considered as the CDs-FITC composites, which implies the successful preparaction of CDs-FITC composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual FL imaging detection of pH and ratiometric FL intensity sensing of pH
ce:section.ce:para: To develop the potential applications of CDs-FITC composites, we carried out a series of visual FL imaging experiments. In Fig. 3a, the aqueous solutions of CDs-FITC composites dispersed in PBS with different pHs show distinct changes in FL-emitting colors. When the pH increases from 3.2 to 9.0, the FL-emitting color varies from deep-blue to deep-green. It means that the pHs in aqueous solutions of composites can be directly indicated with the corresponding FL-emitting colors upon the excitation with a 365nm of UV-lamp. In other words, the composites enable a naked-eye visualization FL imaging of pH. To exploit the application of CDs-FITC composites for ratiometric FL sensing of pH, the FL emission spectra of composites were measured. As shown in Fig. 3b, the FL emission spectra peaked at 518nm (corresponding to FITC) enhance with the increase of pH. By contrast, the FL emission spectra peaked at 412nm (corresponding to CDs) only produce a negligible change. Consequently, the FL of CDs could be used for a reference to fabricate a ratiometric FL sensor of pH, by using CDs-FITC composites as the sensing platform. To find out an ideal pH range, the ratiometric FL intensities (I FITC/I CDs) of CDs-FITC composites were measured under various pH conditions (in Fig. 3a). According to the results in Fig. 3c, the ideal pH range to develop a ratiometric FL sensor of pH should be in pH 4.6–7.7. In Fig. 3d, the I FITC/I CDs increases nearly linearly in the range of pH 4.6–7.7. The linear plotting of I FITC/I CDs versus pH has a good coefficient of 0.998. In general, the pH values of weak acid, weak basic and neuter drink are in this pH range. Therefore, this newly developed CDs-FITC composites-based ratiometric FL sensor could be used for ratiometric FL sensing of pH in drink.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of ratiometric FL sensor of pH in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the analytical performances of this ratiometric FL sensor, the pHs of a group of real samples (50-fold diluted yogurt) were analyzed by this sensor (in Fig. 3d). All analytical results were summarized in Table 1. The detected results of pHs in four different yogurt samples by using this sensor were very close to those obtained from conventional and commecial pH-meter. After six repeated determinations of pHs in yogurt samples by this sensor, the calculated values of RSD are all less than 5%. These results indicate high feasibility, accuracy, stabilization and simplicity of this sensor for pH determination in real yogurt samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL dual-signal intensity sensing and visual FL imaging detection of Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: As reported, PEI (around the surface of CDs in this work) enables a highly selective capture of free Cu2+ ions by –NH2 of PEI to form an absorbent complex, which can lead to the FL quenching of CDs via an inner filter effect [30]. Cu2+ ions are also sensitive to the FL of FITC, and can induce the FL quenching responses of FITC [31]. Therefore, the CDs-FITC composites could be developed toward the FL dual-signal sensing of Cu2+ ions. In our experiments, the FL emission spectra of CDs-FITC composites were recorded in the presence of different [Cu2+] (0–13μM). In Fig. 4a, notably the spectra peaked both at 412nm (for CDs) and 518nm (for FITC) gradually decreased with the increase of [Cu2+]. When the added Cu2+ reached to 13μM, nearly whole FL quenching responses of the composites could be observed at both peak positions. These results indicates that Cu2+ could induce remarkable FL dual-signal quenching of CDs-FITC composites peaked at 412nm and 518nm. Linear plotting of the relative FL intensities (F0-F)/F0 versus [Cu2+] was conducted. As displayed in Fig. 4b, a good linear relationship of (F0-F)/F0 peaked at 412nm and 518nm versus [Cu2+] were plotted in the [Cu2+] range from 1 to 12.5μM, accompanied with good coefficients of 0.991 and 0.989. The limits of detection (LOD) for [Cu2+] were 7nM and 9nM, calculated by the method of a signal-to-noise ratio of S/N=3.

The selectivity and sensitivity of CDs-FITC composites-based FL dual-signal sensing of Cu2+ ions over other cations were evaluated. Other cations were selected and evaluated in interference experiments, as displayed in Fig. 4c. After the addition of each class of cations (12μM), various FL intensity responses of CDs-FITC composites (peaked at 412nm from CDs and 518nm from FITC) were observed. Compared with the addition of Cu2+, other cations only caused negligible changes in the FL intensities peaked both at 412nm and at 518nm. Although the FL decreases at both peaks from the addition of Hg2+ are a little obvious in comparison with other cations, the FL decreases are dramatically less than the case of Cu2+ under identical conditions. Furthermore, the interference from Hg2+ could be also effectively avoided by adding I- in the aqueous suspension of CDs-FITC composites. There is a strong acting force between I- and Hg2+, which could result in the formation of [HgI4]2- that hardly any impacts on the FL of CDs-FITC composites [32]. Hence, these results confirm that this as-developed FL dual-signal sensing is highly selective and sensitive to Cu2+ over other classes of cations.

To evaluate the feasiblity of CDs-FITC composites-based FL dual-signal sensing of Cu2+ ions, its analytical performances were investigated by performing the FL imaging detection experiments of [Cu2+] in different real samples. In this article, this FL dual-signal sensor was used for visual latent fingerprint and leaf FL imaging detection of [Cu2+], the dual-signal FL detection of [Cu2+] in real yogurt and human serum samples. Briefly, the aqueous suspensions of CDs-FITC composites with an increasing [Cu2+] (from 0, 2-5μM) were drop-by-drop added onto the latent fingerprints, which were then irradiated with a 365nm of UV-lamp to observe the FL-emitting colors of latent fingerprint FL imaging. As displayed in Fig. 4d (up), the latent fingerprint FL imaging weakened gradually, so implying that the added aqueous suspension of composites containing an increasing [Cu2+]. Consequently, the results from latent fingerprint FL imaging exhibit a promising potential for visual FL detection of [Cu2+] based on the latent fingerprint FL imaging. Additionally, similar experiments were conducted in leaves. The corresponding results were shown in Fig. 4d (down). After the drop-by-drop addition of aqueous suspensions of composites with an increasing [Cu2+] (0, 2, 5 and 10μM) onto leaves, gradually weakened FL-emitting colors in leaf FL imaging were clearly observed, indicating the potential for [Cu2+] detection by leaf FL imaging. Based on these results above, CDs-FITC composites-based FL sensor of Cu2+ ions shows feasible and promising applications in latent fingerprint and leaf FL imaging. Therefore, this as-developed sensor could be further developed toward the latent fingerprint and leaf FL imaging detection of [Cu2+] in the forthcoming researches.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of FL dual-signal sensor of Cu2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, the analytical performances of CDs-FITC composites-based FL dual-signal sensor of Cu2+ in real yogurt and human serum samples were estimated. As summarized in Table 2, the detected results of [Cu2+] in 50-fold diluted yogurt and human serum samples from this sensor (based on the linear plottings at 412nm from CDs and 518nm from FITC in Fig. 4b) are in good agreement with the fixed values of [Cu2+], together with a narrow RSD range of 2.04–4.76% for CDs (2.16–4.21% for FITC). All measured results from this sensor are close to those obtained from the conventional and commecial ICP-AES technique, which has a broad RSD range from 1.02% to 7.36%. These results adequately confirmed that this as-developed sensor enabled a highly efficient FL dual-signal sensing of Cu2+ ions in real yogurt and human serum samples, together with comparable and even superior analytical performances in comparison with the conventional and commecial techniques in the given [Cu2+] range.

In summary, a facile aqueous synthesis of CDs was developed by using natural kelp as a new class of carbon sources. After direct hydrothermal carbonization of kelp juice, water-dispersible CDs were obtained and then conjugated with PEI to form –NH2 terminated CDs/PEI complex, followed by continuous conjugation with FITC to form CDs-FITC composites. The composites exhibited dual-emissive FL from the characteristic emissions of CDs and FITC. To exploit broad applications of CDs-FITC composites, a series of experiments were conducted, including visual FL imaging of pH in glass bottle, ratiometric FL sensing of pH in real yogurt samples, visual latent fingerprint and leaf FL imaging detection of [Cu2+] in real latent fingerprints and leaves, and dual-signal FL sensing of [Cu2+] in real yogurt and human serum samples. Experimental results confirmed high feasibility, accuracy, stabilization and simplicity of CDs-FITC composites-based sensors of pH and Cu2+. These significant results would promote further development in the fields of facile synthetic methods and broad applications of CDs-based composites with dual or multiple components and microstructures.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21475071, 21405086 and 21275082), the Taishan Scholar Program of Shandong (ts201511027), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong, China (ZR2014BQ001 and BS2014NJ023), and the Qingdao Postdoctoral Applied Research Project (2015136).
